heb.paragraph: "Wail (hēlîlū), you ships of Tarshish! For Tyre is destroyed and left without house or harbour" (v.1). Tyre, the great Phoenician trading port. The ships returning from Tarshish (Spain) learn the news at Cyprus (v.1). The grain of the Nile, the harvest of the Shihor, was Tyre’s revenue (v.3). The sea speaks: "I have neither been in labour nor given birth; I have neither reared sons nor brought up daughters" (v.4) — the sea personified as barren, having produced nothing lasting. "Who planned this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns?" (v.8). Answer: "The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory" (v.9).

notes.note: "Wail, ships of Tarshish!" The Mediterranean trade network collapses. The news arrives at Cyprus: Tyre is gone.
"Who planned this against Tyre? The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory." The theology of every nation-oracle condensed: human pride, divine plan.

sections.header: Verses 10–18 — “After Seventy Years, the LORD Will Deal with Tyre”

heb.paragraph: "Her profit and her earnings will be set apart (qōdesh) for the LORD; they will not be stored up or hoarded. Her profits will go to those who live before the LORD" (v.18). After seventy years (v.15) — the same period as Judah’s exile — Tyre will return to trade. But the stunning twist: Tyre’s profits will be consecrated to the LORD. Commercial wealth redirected to holy purposes. The pagan trading empire becomes a supplier for God’s people. Like Egypt (ch.19) and Cush (ch.18), the enemy’s resources are repurposed for God’s kingdom.

notes.note: "At the end of seventy years, the LORD will deal with Tyre. She will return to her lucrative prostitution." Tyre’s trade is described as prostitution — selling herself to all comers. Revelation 17–18 develops this Tyre/Babylon-as-prostitute imagery.
"Her profit will be set apart for the LORD." The surprise: not destruction but consecration. Pagan wealth redirected to holy use. The pattern: judgment → restoration → service to God.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: "Who planned this against Tyre? The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVOn the great waters came the grain of the Shihor; the harvest of the Nile was the revenue of Tyre, and she became the marketplace of the nations.ESVAnd on many waters your revenue was the grain of Shihor, the harvest of the Nile; you were the merchant of the nations.NIVWho planned this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns, whose merchants are princes, whose traders are renowned in the earth?ESVWho has purposed this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns, whose merchants were princes, whose traders were the honored of the earth?

rec.text: ""Wail, ships of Tarshish!" The Mediterranean trade network collapses. The news arrives at Cyprus: Tyre is gone."

rows.label: Pride Brought Down — 23:1–9
rows.text: "Wail, ships!" "The LORD planned it, to bring down the PRIDE of all glory."

hebtext.note: "Wail (hēlîlū), you ships of Tarshish! For Tyre is destroyed and left without house or harbour" (v.1). Tyre, the great Phoenician trading port. The ships returning from Tarshish (Spain) learn the news at Cyprus (v.1). The grain of the Nile, the harvest of the Shihor, was Tyre’s revenue (v.3). The sea speaks: "I have neither been in labour nor given birth; I have neither reared sons nor brought up daughters" (v.4) — the sea personified as barren, having produced nothing lasting. "Who planned this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns?" (v.8). Answer: "The LORD Almighty planned it, to bring down the pride of all glory" (v.9).

"Her profit and her earnings will be set apart (qōdesh) for the LORD; they will not be stored up or hoarded. Her profits will go to those who live before the LORD" (v.18). After seventy years (v.15) — the same period as Judah’s exile — Tyre will return to trade. But the stunning twist: Tyre’s profits will be consecrated to the LORD. Commercial wealth redirected to holy purposes. The pagan trading empire becomes a supplier for God’s people. Like Egypt (ch.19) and Cush (ch.18), the enemy’s resources are repurposed for God’s kingdom.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: God plans Tyre’s fall and Tyre’s consecration. The oracles-against-nations section ends with the pattern complete: judgment, then surprising inclusion.
